claim.content: The empirical dimensionless prefactor obtained from the fits, p ≈ 13.6, is substantially larger than the value p = 2π ≈ 6.28 predicted by a simple isotropic three-dimensional effective-mass Fermi-liquid model, and this discrepancy indicates that one or more of the following modify the numerical prefactor entering the Fermi-liquid damping in layered, quasi-two-dimensional TiS₂: anisotropy of the Fermi surface, limited momentum-relaxing umklapp scattering in small Fermi pockets, uncertainties in the effective mass, or disorder/stacking-fault scattering.

conclusion.content: The empirical dimensionless prefactor obtained from the fits, p ≈ 13.6, is substantially larger than the value p = 2π ≈ 6.28 predicted by a simple isotropic three-dimensional effective-mass Fermi-liquid model, and this discrepancy indicates that one or more of the following modify the numerical prefactor entering the Fermi-liquid damping in layered, quasi-two-dimensional TiS₂: anisotropy of the Fermi surface, limited momentum-relaxing umklapp scattering in small Fermi pockets, uncertainties in the effective mass, or disorder/stacking-fault scattering.

premises.content:
The empirical dimensionless prefactor obtained from fits of the relaxation rate τ⁻¹(T,ω)=α[(pT)²+ω²] is p ≈ 13.6, which exceeds the isotropic-model expectation p = 2π, and the paper interprets this discrepancy as plausibly arising from one or more of the following in layered TiS₂: anisotropy of the Fermi surface, restricted momentum-relaxing umklapp scattering in small Fermi pockets, uncertainties in the effective mass value used in the analysis, or additional elastic scattering from disorder or stacking faults.
      Fig. 2
      Eq. (1)

The numerical prediction p = 2π for the relative magnitude of the temperature- and frequency-dependent scattering terms in τ⁻¹ derives from an isotropic three-dimensional effective-mass Fermi-liquid model and therefore assumes isotropy and a particular umklapp phase-space; these assumptions need not hold for quasi-two-dimensional, small-pocket Fermi surfaces such as those expected in layered TiS₂, so the isotropic-model numeric prediction may not be applicable to this material.
      R.N. Gurzhi (1958), J.M. Luttinger (1961) (theoretical background cited in paper)

steps.reasoning:
Take as established the empirically fitted value $p\approx 13.6$ (dimensionless) for the prefactor appearing in the fitted scattering rate $1/\tau(T,\omega)=\alpha\big[(pT)^{2}+\omega^{2}\big]$, as obtained from the optical reflectivity fits and documented in the analysis.
Fig. 2

State the theoretical expectation for a simple isotropic three-dimensional effective-mass Fermi-liquid model that the corresponding numerical prefactor should be $p=2\pi\approx 6.28$, citing the conventional Fermi-liquid analyses referenced by the authors.
[4,5]

Compare the empirical value $p\approx 13.6$ with the isotropic-model expectation $p=2\pi$ and observe that the empirical prefactor is substantially larger (approximately a factor of two or more), creating a quantitative discrepancy between simple isotropic theory and experiment.
Fig. 3

Enumerate the possible material-specific factors, as discussed by the authors, that could modify the numerical prefactor entering the Fermi-liquid damping: (i) anisotropy of the Fermi surface in the quasi-two-dimensional TiS$_{2}$ (which alters phase space and scattering matrix elements), (ii) limitations of momentum-relaxing umklapp scattering due to small Fermi-surface pockets, (iii) uncertainties in the effective mass $m^{*}$ differing from the free-electron mass $m_{0}$, and (iv) disorder effects such as stacking faults or small non-stoichiometry $\delta$ in $Ti_{1+\delta}S_{2}$ that affect resistivity and scattering.

Conclude that the substantially larger empirical prefactor $p\approx 13.6$ indicates that simple isotropic-model predictions are inadequate for TiS$_{2}$ and that Fermi-surface anisotropy, restricted umklapp phase space, effective-mass renormalization, or disorder-related effects likely modify the numerical prefactor entering the observed Fermi-liquid damping in this material, without assigning a unique cause among these possibilities.
Fig. 2

motivating_questions.content: Theoretical Fermi-liquid predictions for the relative magnitudes of temperature- and frequency-dependent scattering terms yield specific numerical prefactors (e.g., $p=2\pi$ in simple isotropic models). In anisotropic layered compounds with small carrier pockets, the numerical factors controlling $\tau^{-1}\propto (pT)^{2}+\omega^{2}$ can differ substantially, but before this work there was no experimental determination of $p$ for TiS$_{2}$. Determining whether deviations from isotropic-model predictions arise from Fermi-surface anisotropy, restricted umklapp phase space, effective-mass renormalization, or disorder is necessary to reconcile microscopic theory with observed transport coefficients.
